prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 29-year-old man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: For 3 days I've noticed lots of new tiny red/purple spots on my legs and my gums bleed when I brush. I had a nosebleed today that took 15 minutes to stop.
- History: I had a viral illness about 2 weeks ago. No blood thinners and no liver disease. Only took a few doses of ibuprofen.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <Your symptoms—many new tiny red/purple spots (petechiae), bleeding gums, and a nosebleed that took a long time to stop—suggest low platelets (thrombocytopenia), which can follow viral infections (immune thrombocytopenia). Low platelets make you bruise and bleed more easily and can, in some cases, lead to dangerous bleeding. You need a blood test (complete blood count) and clinical assessment soon to know how low the platelets are and whether treatment is needed. Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin and heavy activity until evaluated. Go to the ER right away only if you develop heavy or uncontrolled bleeding, bloody/black stools, vomiting blood, fainting, severe headache, or weakness.>

TRIAGE: <C>

CONFIDENCE: <80%>

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): The new tiny red/purple spots on your legs are likely petechiae/purpura (small skin bleeds). Bleeding from gums and a nosebleed that took 15 minutes to stop means mucosal bleeding. Together these signs suggest your blood isn’t clotting normally, most often because platelet numbers are low — sometimes triggered by a viral illness (immune thrombocytopenia). This can be mild but can also become dangerous if platelet counts are very low. You need a same-day blood count and medical assessment to check platelet level and stop or monitor bleeding. Go to the ER immediately if bleeding won’t stop, you feel faint, have fast heart rate, severe headache, vomiting blood, or bloody/black stools or urine.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): The new tiny red/purple spots on your legs (petechiae), bleeding gums and a nosebleed that took 15 minutes to stop suggest a problem with clotting, most commonly a low platelet count (thrombocytopenia). That can follow viral infections and can range from mild to severe; with mucosal bleeding it may be significant. You need a blood test (complete blood count with platelet count) promptly to know how low the platelets are and whether treatment is needed. Until checked, avoid NSAIDs/aspirin and activities that risk injury. Go to the emergency department right away if you develop heavy or uncontrolled bleeding, weakness, fainting, shortness of breath, bloody/black stools or vomiting blood.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your new tiny red/purple spots (petechiae), bleeding gums when brushing, and a nosebleed that took 15 minutes to stop strongly suggest your platelets — the blood cells that stop bleeding — may be low. This can follow viral illnesses (immune thrombocytopenia) and raises the risk of more serious bleeding. You should get a complete blood count (CBC) promptly to check platelet levels and basic clotting. If platelets are very low, doctors can start treatments to reduce bleeding risk. Go to the ER right away if you develop heavy or uncontrolled bleeding, vomiting blood, black/tarry stools, fainting, severe headache, or trouble breathing.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): These new tiny red/purple spots (petechiae), easy gum bleeding and a nosebleed that took a long time to stop suggest your blood is not clotting normally — most commonly because platelets are low. A viral illness can trigger an immune drop in platelets (immune thrombocytopenia), which can cause exactly these symptoms. Because mucosal bleeding (gums, nose) and widespread spots can mean platelet counts are low enough to risk more serious bleeding, you need a quick blood test (CBC) and medical assessment to measure platelets and decide treatment. Watch for heavy or ongoing bleeding, blood in stool/urine, fainting or breathlessness.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — many new tiny red/purple spots (petechiae), bleeding gums, and a nosebleed after a recent viral illness — are most consistent with a low platelet count (for example immune thrombocytopenia). Platelets stop bleeding; when they’re low you get mucosal bleeding and petechiae. This can be mild but can become dangerous if platelet counts are very low. You should get a complete blood count (CBC) urgently to check platelet level and basic clotting tests. Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin, heavy exertion, and dental procedures until evaluated. Go to the ER now if you have heavy or uncontrolled bleeding, fainting, shortness of breath, or weakness.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — many new tiny red/purple spots on the skin (petechiae), bleeding gums, and a nosebleed that took 15 minutes to stop — strongly suggest a low platelet count (which can follow a viral illness). Low platelets reduce clotting and can cause mucosal bleeding and easy bruising. This can be serious if the platelet count is very low because bleeding can become hard to control. You should stop taking NSAIDs (ibuprofen) and avoid activities that risk injury until evaluated. The next step is an urgent blood test (CBC) to check platelet levels and prompt medical assessment to determine cause and treatment.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your new tiny red/purple spots (petechiae), bleeding gums with brushing, and a nosebleed that took 15 minutes to stop are most consistent with a bleeding problem from low platelets (thrombocytopenia). This can follow a viral illness and sometimes is immune-mediated. Low platelets raise the risk of more serious bleeding and need a blood test (CBC) to measure the platelet count quickly. Stop NSAIDs (like ibuprofen) and avoid aspirin or heavy exercise until evaluated. Go to urgent evaluation sooner if you develop heavy or ongoing bleeding, fainting, shortness of breath, bloody/black stools or urine, or weakness.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): The new tiny red/purple spots (petechiae), bleeding gums when brushing, and a nosebleed that took 15 minutes to stop all suggest a problem with clotting platelets (low platelet count). This can follow a viral illness and may be caused by immune-mediated platelet destruction (for example, ITP) or other causes. Because mucosal bleeding and petechiae can mean platelet levels are low enough to cause significant bleeding, you need urgent blood tests (CBC with platelet count) and clinical assessment. In the meantime avoid NSAIDs/aspirin, avoid heavy activity, and do not delay evaluation.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — many new tiny red/purple spots on the legs (petechiae/purpura) plus bleeding gums and a nosebleed that took a long time to stop — most commonly point to a low platelet count (thrombocytopenia), which can follow a viral illness (immune thrombocytopenia/ITP). Low platelets make small blood vessels leak and raise the risk of more serious bleeding. This is potentially urgent because of the mucosal bleeding you describe. Stop NSAIDs like ibuprofen (they can worsen bleeding), avoid heavy exertion, and get a complete blood count promptly. If bleeding becomes heavy, you vomit or cough up blood, pass bloody/black stool or urine, develop severe headache/confusion, or can’t control nose/mouth bleeding, seek emergency care.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 80%
